import cv2
import base64
from google import genai
from google.genai.types import LiveConnectConfig

client = genai.Client(
    vertexai=True,
    project="your-project-id",
    location="us-central1"
)

MODEL = "gemini-2.5-flash-live-preview"

async def video_chat():
    config = LiveConnectConfig(
        response_modalities=["TEXT"],
        system_instruction="You are an AI assistant that analyzes video. Describe what you see clearly and concisely."
    )

    cap = cv2.VideoCapture(0)  # Open the default camera

    async with client.aio.live.connect(model=MODEL, config=config) as session:
        async def send_video_frames():
            """Send camera frames at regular intervals"""
            while True:
                ret, frame = cap.read()
                if not ret:
                    break

                # Encode the frame as JPEG
                _, buffer = cv2.imencode(".jpg", frame)
                image_data = base64.b64encode(buffer).decode("utf-8")

                # Send the video frame
                await session.send(
                    input={
                        "mime_type": "image/jpeg",
                        "data": image_data
                    }
                )
                await asyncio.sleep(0.5)  # Send a frame every 0.5 seconds

        async def receive_responses():
            """Receive and print text responses"""
            async for response in session.receive():
                if response.text:
                    print(f"AI: {response.text}")

        await asyncio.gather(send_video_frames(), receive_responses())

    cap.release()

asyncio.run(video_chat())

Customizing with System Instructions

You can fine-tune the AI's behavior using system_instruction in your config.

config = LiveConnectConfig(
    response_modalities=["AUDIO"],
    system_instruction="""
    You are a friendly customer support AI for a software product.
    - Always be polite and professional.
    - Provide clear, step-by-step instructions for technical questions.
    - If you don't know the answer, acknowledge it honestly and offer to transfer to a human agent.
    """,
    speech_config=SpeechConfig(
        voice_config=VoiceConfig(
            prebuilt_voice_config=PrebuiltVoiceConfig(voice_name="Kore")
        )
    )
)

FAQ

Q: How is the Gemini Live API priced?

A: Pricing on Vertex AI is based on the number of tokens processed for audio input and output. Billing officially began on January 28, 2026. See the Vertex AI Pricing page for current rates.

Q: What languages are supported?

A: The Live API supports a wide range of languages, including robust support for English and Japanese in both speech recognition and speech synthesis.

Q: Is there a maximum session duration?

A: Yes, the maximum session duration per connection is 15 minutes. For longer interactions, implement a reconnection strategy in your application.

Q: Can I still use the Vertex AI SDK?

A: Vertex AI SDK releases after June 2026 will no longer support Gemini. New Gemini features are only available in the Google Gen AI SDK. Migrating is straightforward — the main change is switching from the google-cloud-aiplatform package to google-genai.

Q: What kind of latency can I expect?

A: In general, you can expect 100ms–300ms of latency. This can vary based on your network conditions and the GCP region you're connecting to.

Summary

The Gemini Live API dramatically simplifies building real-time multimodal conversational AI that combines voice, video, and text. Its GA availability on Vertex AI makes it ready for production-grade, enterprise-scale deployments.

Key takeaways:

  • Use the Gen AI SDK (google-genai) to access Gemini Live API on Vertex AI
  • Supports audio-only, text-only, or combined audio + video configurations
  • Customize AI behavior with system_instruction for domain-specific applications
  • Billing started January 28, 2026 — plan your pricing model accordingly

As a next step, consider building out real-world use cases such as call center automation, accessibility tools, or interactive educational tutors.

en.title: Gemini 3 Flash Complete Guide — Frontier AI at Flash-Level Speed and Lower Cost
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: A complete guide to Google's Gemini 3 Flash: features, benchmarks, pricing, and how to get started. Covers 1M context, multimodal input, and configurable reasoning.
en.content:
Introduction

In March 2026, Google officially released Gemini 3 Flash — a next-generation AI model designed to deliver frontier intelligence at Flash-level speed and cost. This model significantly outperforms the 2.5 series while keeping pricing accessible for developers and enterprises alike.

This guide covers Gemini 3 Flash's capabilities, benchmarks, pricing, and practical usage examples for developers.

What Is Gemini 3 Flash?

Gemini 3 Flash is Google's latest large language model that redefines what "Flash" means in the Gemini lineup. Rather than trading off performance for speed, Gemini 3 Flash delivers Pro-grade reasoning at Flash-level latency and cost.

Key capabilities include:

  • 1 million token context window: Process massive codebases, documents, and conversations in a single call
  • Multimodal input support: Text, images, audio, video, and PDFs
  • Configurable reasoning levels: Choose from minimal, low, medium, or high thinking depth
  • Automatic context caching: Reduce costs on repeated processing
  • Structured output and tool use: Built for production agentic applications

Benchmark Performance

Gemini 3 Flash sets a new bar for performance in its class, surpassing many frontier models including its Pro counterpart.

| Benchmark | Score | Description | |-----------|-------|-------------| | GPQA Diamond | 90.4% | PhD-level reasoning and knowledge | | Humanity's Last Exam | 33.7% | Hardest exam without tools | | SWE-bench Verified | 78% | Agentic software engineering |

Notably, its SWE-bench Verified score of 78% surpasses Gemini 3 Pro on coding tasks, making it the top choice for software development use cases.

Pricing

Gemini 3 Flash is available through the Gemini API and Vertex AI at the following rates:

| Type | Price | |------|-------| | Input tokens | $0.50 / 1M tokens | | Output tokens | $3.00 / 1M tokens |

Compared to Gemini 2.5 Pro ($1.25/1M input), Gemini 3 Flash cuts input costs by more than half, making it an economical choice for high-volume applications.

How to Use Gemini 3 Flash

Try It in Google AI Studio

The quickest way to get started is Google AI Studio. Select gemini-3-flash-preview from the model selector and start prompting — no API key required for testing.

Gemini API (Python)

import google.generativeai as genai

genai.configure(api_key="YOUR_API_KEY")

model = genai.GenerativeModel("gemini-3-flash-preview")

response = model.generate_content(
    "Write a Python function to generate the Fibonacci sequence."
)
print(response.text)

Setting Reasoning Levels

Gemini 3 Flash lets you control thinking depth across four levels. Use higher reasoning for complex tasks and lower for latency-sensitive applications.

from google.generativeai.types import GenerationConfig

config = GenerationConfig(
    thinking_config={"thinking_budget": "high"}  # minimal / low / medium / high
)

response = model.generate_content(
    "Solve the following complex math problem: ...",
    generation_config=config
)
print(response.text)

Multimodal Input (Images)

import PIL.Image

image = PIL.Image.open("screenshot.png")

response = model.generate_content(
    ["Identify the bug in this screenshot and suggest a fix.", image]
)
print(response.text)

Gemini CLI Integration

Gemini 3 Flash is also available in the Gemini CLI for terminal-based workflows.

# Install Gemini CLI (if not already installed)
npm install -g @google/gemini-cli

# Run with Gemini 3 Flash
gemini --model gemini-3-flash-preview "How can I improve the performance of this code?"

Best Use Cases

Here are the scenarios where Gemini 3 Flash truly shines.

Code generation and review: With a 78% SWE-bench score, it excels at bug fixing, refactoring, and code review — often outperforming dedicated coding models.

Document analysis at scale: The 1M token context makes it ideal for processing large PDFs, financial reports, or legal documents in a single request.

Multimodal applications: Build real-time apps that combine text, images, and audio at low cost and high speed.

Agentic development: With built-in tool use and function calling, it integrates naturally into Google Antigravity and other agentic platforms.

Games and interactive experiences: Low latency and high reasoning make it suitable for game AI and interactive characters.

Gemini 3 Flash vs. Gemini 2.5 Pro

| Feature | Gemini 3 Flash | Gemini 2.5 Pro | |---------|---------------|----------------| | Input cost | $0.50/1M | $1.25/1M | | SWE-bench | 78% | ~63% | | GPQA Diamond | 90.4% | ~84% | | Speed | ◎ Fast | ○ Moderate | | Reasoning levels | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | Context window | 1M | 1M |

For most new projects, Gemini 3 Flash is the better starting point given its superior performance at a lower price point.

FAQ

Q: Is Gemini 3 Flash publicly available? A: As of March 2026, it is available to developers through the Gemini API and Vertex AI, and to general users through the Gemini app.

Q: Is there a free tier? A: Google AI Studio offers a free tier with usage limits. Check the official AI Studio documentation for the latest quota information.

Q: Can I migrate from Gemini 2.5 Flash with minimal changes? A: Yes. Simply change the model name to gemini-3-flash-preview — the API interface remains the same.

Q: What's the difference between Gemini 3 Flash and Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite? A: Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite is a lighter, faster, and more cost-efficient variant optimized for simple tasks. Use 3.1 Flash Lite for lightweight processing and 3 Flash when higher performance is needed.

Summary

Gemini 3 Flash is Google's latest model released in March 2026, delivering frontier-level intelligence at Flash speed and cost.

  • SWE-bench 78% and GPQA Diamond 90.4% set new performance benchmarks
  • $0.50/1M input token pricing offers strong cost efficiency
  • Four configurable reasoning levels for flexible task handling
  • Full multimodal support with a 1M token context window

If you're starting a new AI project, Gemini 3 Flash is the model to try first. Get started for free in Google AI Studio today.

en.title: Build Web Apps with Gemini 2.5 Pro — Testing the WebDev Arena #1
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: Gemini 2.5 Pro ranked #1 on WebDev Arena. Learn how to leverage its web app generation capabilities with practical prompting tips and development workflows.
en.content:
Building Web Apps with the #1 Ranked AI

Gemini 2.5 Pro has claimed the top spot on the WebDev Arena leaderboard — a benchmark that measures AI models' ability to build aesthetically pleasing and functional web apps based on human preference.

This ranking reflects Gemini 2.5 Pro's exceptional quality in web app generation. This article shows you how to get the most out of it.

Development Flow from Google AI Studio

Step 1: Prototype in AI Studio

Go to Google AI Studio and select Gemini 2.5 Pro.

Build a simple Pomodoro timer web app.
Requirements:
- 25-minute work / 5-minute break countdown
- Display remaining time with a large circular progress bar
- Sound notification on session completion
- Dark theme
- Responsive design (desktop and mobile)
- Single HTML file

Gemini 2.5 Pro generates a fully working web app from this prompt alone.

Step 2: Iterate

Review the generated app and refine through chat:

Add the option to choose between 25-minute and 50-minute work sessions.
Also show today's completed Pomodoro count at the bottom of the screen.

Step 3: Deploy

Transfer your code from AI Studio to Antigravity, or deploy directly to Cloudflare Pages or Vercel.

Prompting Best Practices

Be Specific About Requirements

❌ "Build a weather app"
✅ "Build a web app that shows the weather for my current location.
   Use OpenWeather API.
   Display temperature, humidity, weather icon, and 3-day forecast.
   Use glassmorphism card design.
   Single HTML file."

Specify Design Style

Gemini 2.5 Pro responds well to design direction:

  • "Glassmorphism," "Neumorphism," "Minimalist"
  • "Apple-style," "Material Design"
  • Color palette (e.g., "Use #667EEA as the primary color")
  • Animations (e.g., "Fade in," "Slide transitions")

Build Incrementally

For larger apps, add features step by step:

  1. Start with a basic MVP
  2. Refine the UI design
  3. Add features incrementally
  4. Improve responsiveness and accessibility

Where Gemini 2.5 Pro Shines

Single-File Apps

Gemini excels at generating self-contained HTML/CSS/JavaScript apps. Tools, dashboards, calculators, games — it handles a wide range of categories.

Visual Quality

One reason it tops WebDev Arena is the beauty of its generated UIs. Expect polished designs with effective use of gradients, shadows, and animations.

Interactivity

It can generate apps with advanced interactions including drag-and-drop, keyboard shortcuts, and real-time previews.

Practical Example: Sales Dashboard

Build a sales dashboard web app.

Requirements:
- Dark theme, sidebar navigation
- KPI cards: total revenue, order count, average order value, month-over-month change
- Monthly revenue line chart (using Chart.js)
- Regional sales pie chart
- Recent orders table
- Include demo data
- Single HTML file

Takeaway

Gemini 2.5 Pro's web app generation capability is exceptional, as the benchmark results confirm. By providing specific requirements and design direction in your prompts, you can generate professional web apps in minutes. Try it for free on Google AI Studio.

en.title: Gemini API Logging & Datasets Tool — A Practical Guide
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: Learn how to use the new Gemini API logging and datasets tool. Record API requests, analyze usage patterns, and streamline model evaluation.
en.content:
A New Way to Monitor Your API Usage

In March 2026, Google released a new logging and datasets tool for the Gemini API. This tool lets you record, analyze, and manage API request data directly from the Google AI Studio dashboard.

Previously, developers had to build their own logging infrastructure. Now, API usage monitoring is a built-in feature.

Key Features

Automatic Request Logging

API calls are automatically logged with both request and response data:

  • Input prompts (text, images, video)
  • Output responses
  • Token usage
  • Latency
  • Model name and version
  • Error information

Dataset Creation and Management

Create datasets from logs for model evaluation or fine-tuning:

import google.generativeai as genai

genai.configure(api_key="YOUR_API_KEY")

# Create dataset from logs
dataset = genai.datasets.create(
    name="customer-support-logs",
    description="Customer support API call logs",
    source="logs",
    filter={
        "date_range": {"start": "2026-03-01", "end": "2026-03-12"},
        "model": "gemini-2.5-pro"
    }
)

Filtering and Search

Quickly find specific requests in large volumes of logs:

  • Date range filtering
  • Model version filtering
  • Response quality score filtering
  • Text search (search within prompt content)

Use Cases

Prompt Quality Improvement

Analyze logs to quantitatively understand which prompts produce good results and which fall short of expectations.

Cost Optimization

Review token usage at the request level to identify expensive calls. Optimize through prompt trimming or model selection (Pro vs Flash).

Model Evaluation

Run the same prompt set against different model versions for easy A/B testing:

# Model comparison using datasets
evaluation = genai.evaluations.run(
    dataset="customer-support-logs",
    models=["gemini-2.5-pro", "gemini-2.5-flash"],
    metrics=["quality", "latency", "cost"]
)

print(evaluation.summary())

Anomaly Detection

Detect sudden changes in response quality or spikes in error rates for early problem identification.

Viewing in Google AI Studio

  1. Go to Google AI Studio
  2. Select "Logs & Datasets" from the left menu
  3. Review log summaries on the dashboard
  4. Apply filters for detailed analysis

Privacy and Security

  • Log data is scoped to your project and inaccessible to other users
  • Logs containing sensitive data are stored encrypted
  • Default retention period is 30 days (customizable)
  • Operates in GDPR and SOC2 compliant environments

Things to Note

  • The logging feature is currently in beta
  • Large log volumes may incur storage costs
  • Real-time log streaming is planned for a future update

Takeaway

The Gemini API logging and datasets tool is essential for developers running the API in production. It enables meaningful improvements across three key dimensions: prompt quality, cost optimization, and model evaluation.

en.title: Gemini 2.5 Pro TTS — The New Era of AI Voice Synthesis
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: Explore Gemini 2.5 Pro TTS preview — featuring enhanced expressivity, precision pacing, and seamless dialogue. Learn how to use the new TTS model in your projects.
en.content:
Gemini Enters the Voice Synthesis Game

In March 2026, Google launched the Gemini 2.5 Pro TTS preview — a new text-to-speech model optimized for quality. It represents a significant leap beyond traditional Google Cloud TTS in expressiveness and naturalness.

While previous TTS models were tools that converted text to audio, Gemini 2.5 Pro TTS is an AI that understands the intent of text and speaks naturally.

Key Features

Enhanced Expressivity

The model understands emotion and nuance, automatically applying context-appropriate intonation. Whether it's reading news, narrating a story, or delivering a business presentation, the tone adjusts accordingly.

Precision Pacing

It understands sentence structure and inserts pauses at the right places — not just at commas and periods, but at semantic boundaries, creating a natural speaking rhythm.

Seamless Dialogue

The ability to naturally differentiate speakers in conversational text has been dramatically improved. In multi-speaker dialogues, each character gets distinct vocal qualities and tones.

Using the API

Basic TTS Request

import google.generativeai as genai

genai.configure(api_key="YOUR_API_KEY")

model = genai.GenerativeModel("gemini-2.5-pro-tts-preview")

response = model.generate_content(
    "Read the following text naturally: "
    "It's a beautiful day today. Perfect weather for a walk.",
    generation_config={
        "response_modalities": ["audio"],
        "speech_config": {
            "voice_config": {
                "prebuilt_voice_config": {
                    "voice_name": "Kore"
                }
            }
        }
    }
)

# Save audio data
with open("output.wav", "wb") as f:
    f.write(response.candidates[0].content.parts[0].inline_data.data)

Style Control

Control the reading tone through prompts:

prompt = """
Read the following news script like a professional anchor.
Use a calm voice and slow down for important parts:

Google announced new TTS capabilities for Gemini 2.5 Pro today.
This feature enables developers to easily integrate
high-quality voice synthesis into their applications.
"""

Use Cases

Auto-Generated Podcasts

Convert blog posts and reports into podcast-style audio content. Similar to NotebookLM's Audio Overview, but directly accessible via API.

Multilingual Apps

When building apps with voice output in Japanese, English, Chinese, and more, Gemini TTS delivers native-quality speech for each language.

Accessibility

Great potential for screen readers and educational tools. The audio quality is significantly more listenable compared to traditional TTS.

Compared to Cloud TTS

| Feature | Gemini 2.5 Pro TTS | Cloud TTS | |---------|-------------------|-----------| | Emotion | Auto-adjusts from context | Manual SSML tags | | Dialogue | Natural speaker separation | Single speaker | | Customization | Prompt-based | SSML-based | | Pricing | API pay-as-you-go | Per-character |

Things to Note

  • Currently in preview — exercise caution for production use
  • Real-time audio streaming is planned for a future update
  • Audio quality may vary for some languages

Takeaway

Gemini 2.5 Pro TTS sets a new standard for AI voice synthesis. Its prompt-based style control and context-aware natural speech are its standout features. It's easy to try through the API — give it a spin on your next project.

en.title: Gemini 3.1 Pro Complete Guide: Mastering Complex Tasks with the Three-Tier Thinking System
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: A complete guide to Gemini 3.1 Pro, released February 2026. Explore the three-tier thinking system, 77.1% ARC-AGI-2 reasoning score, 1M token context, and hands-on API examples.
en.content:
Introduction

On February 19, 2026, Google released Gemini 3.1 Pro as a public preview — the latest and most capable model in the Gemini family. This release brings two headline improvements over Gemini 3 Pro: a dramatic leap in reasoning performance and a new three-tier thinking system that gives developers fine-grained control over computation depth.

In this guide, we'll walk through what makes Gemini 3.1 Pro special and how to start using it today.

What Is Gemini 3.1 Pro?

Gemini 3.1 Pro is Google DeepMind's flagship model designed for complex problem-solving, agentic workflows, and advanced coding tasks. It's available across Gemini API (AI Studio), Vertex AI, Gemini Enterprise, Gemini CLI, and Android Studio.

Model Specs at a Glance

| Spec | Details | |------|---------| | Model ID | gemini-3.1-pro-preview | | Released | February 19, 2026 | | Context Window | Up to 1,000,000 tokens | | Supported Modalities | Text, images, audio, video, PDFs, code repositories | | Reasoning (ARC-AGI-2) | 77.1% — more than double Gemini 3 Pro |

The Headline Feature: Three-Tier Thinking System

The most important addition in Gemini 3.1 Pro is the three-tier thinking budget system. Previous Gemini models offered only a binary choice between fast (low compute) and deep (high compute) modes. Gemini 3.1 Pro introduces a Medium tier, allowing developers to strike a mathematically balanced trade-off between response latency and reasoning depth.

Choosing the Right Thinking Mode

| Mode | Characteristics | Best For | |------|----------------|----------| | Low | Fast, low cost | Q&A, text generation, simple tasks | | Medium | Balanced | Summarization, code review, standard reasoning | | High | Deep, precise | Math proofs, complex agent workflows |

Getting Started: Calling the API

Basic Python Example

import google.generativeai as genai

genai.configure(api_key="YOUR_API_KEY")

# Specify Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview
model = genai.GenerativeModel("gemini-3.1-pro-preview")

response = model.generate_content(
    "Explain the basics of quantum computing for a beginner.",
    generation_config=genai.GenerationConfig(
        temperature=0.7,
        max_output_tokens=2048,
    )
)

print(response.text)

Specifying the Thinking Budget

import google.generativeai as genai

genai.configure(api_key="YOUR_API_KEY")

model = genai.GenerativeModel(
    "gemini-3.1-pro-preview",
    generation_config={
        "thinking_config": {
            "thinking_budget": "medium"  # "low" / "medium" / "high"
        }
    }
)

response = model.generate_content(
    "Analyze the pattern in this sequence and predict the next 5 values: 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21"
)

print(response.text)

REST API Call

curl -X POST \
  "https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/models/gemini-3.1-pro-preview:generateContent?key=YOUR_API_KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "contents": [{
      "parts": [{"text": "What are the best practices for implementing async processing in Python?"}]
    }],
    "generationConfig": {
      "thinkingConfig": {
        "thinkingBudget": "high"
      }
    }
  }'

Reasoning Breakthrough: 77.1% on ARC-AGI-2

Gemini 3.1 Pro achieved 77.1% on ARC-AGI-2, a rigorous benchmark designed to test genuine general reasoning — patterns a model has never seen before. This is more than double the score of Gemini 3 Pro and represents a top-tier result at the time of release.

ARC-AGI-2 is considered especially hard because it demands novel pattern recognition and logic that can't be memorized — making this score a meaningful signal that AI is approaching more flexible, human-like reasoning.

Multimodal Capabilities in Practice

Gemini 3.1 Pro handles text, images, audio, video, PDFs, and full code repositories through its unified 1M-token context window.

Analyzing an Image

import google.generativeai as genai
import PIL.Image

genai.configure(api_key="YOUR_API_KEY")

model = genai.GenerativeModel("gemini-3.1-pro-preview")

# Load a local image
image = PIL.Image.open("architecture_diagram.png")

response = model.generate_content([
    "Analyze this diagram in detail and suggest any improvements.",
    image
])

print(response.text)

Parsing a PDF Document

import google.generativeai as genai

genai.configure(api_key="YOUR_API_KEY")

# Upload the PDF
uploaded_file = genai.upload_file("quarterly_report.pdf", mime_type="application/pdf")

model = genai.GenerativeModel("gemini-3.1-pro-preview")

response = model.generate_content([
    "Summarize the three key takeaways from this report and suggest actionable next steps.",
    uploaded_file
])

print(response.text)

Where to Access Gemini 3.1 Pro

For Developers

  • Google AI Studio — Try it free in preview
  • Gemini API (gemini-3.1-pro-preview)
  • Vertex AI (enterprise-grade)
  • Gemini CLI

For End Users

  • Gemini app (Google AI Pro / Ultra plans)
  • NotebookLM (Pro / Ultra plans only)
  • GitHub Copilot (public preview)

FAQ

Q: How is Gemini 3.1 Pro different from Gemini 2.5 Pro? A: 3.1 Pro is a newer generation entirely — part of the Gemini 3 series that succeeded the 2.x line. Key differences include the three-tier thinking system and significantly improved reasoning benchmarks.

Q: Is it free to use? A: You can access it for free via Google AI Studio with an API key during the preview period, subject to rate limits.

Q: When will gemini-3.1-pro-preview become generally available? A: Google is validating the preview before a full GA release. Check the official changelog for the latest updates.

Q: Does Gemini 3.1 Pro support languages other than English? A: Yes — it supports multiple languages including Japanese, with high-quality, natural-sounding output in each.

Conclusion

Gemini 3.1 Pro sets a new bar with its three-tier thinking system and unprecedented ARC-AGI-2 reasoning scores. Whether you're building agentic pipelines, multimodal applications, or tackling complex analytical tasks, this model gives you the flexibility and power to handle it all.

Get started by grabbing an API key from Google AI Studio and experimenting with gemini-3.1-pro-preview. Try switching between thinking modes to find the right balance of speed and depth for your specific use case.

References: Gemini 3.1 Pro Official Blog / Gemini API Docs

en.title: Getting Started with Gemini 3 Computer Use — AI That Operates Your Browser
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: Learn how to use the Computer Use tool in Gemini 3 Flash and Pro. Build AI agents that autonomously navigate browsers and UIs — with code examples and real-world scenarios.
en.content:
Getting Started with Gemini 3 Computer Use — AI That Operates Your Browser

Introduction

In March 2026, Google introduced Computer Use tool support for Gemini 3 Flash Preview and Gemini 3 Pro Preview. This breakthrough enables developers to build computer-operating agents — AI systems that can look at a screenshot and autonomously navigate a browser or desktop UI by clicking, typing, and scrolling just like a human would.

This is fundamentally different from traditional browser automation tools like Selenium or Playwright scripting. Rather than following a rigid set of pre-programmed instructions, Gemini's Computer Use understands the content of the screen and decides on the most appropriate action in real time, adapting to unexpected changes and dynamic interfaces.

In this guide, we'll cover everything you need to know: what Computer Use is, which models support it, how to implement it, and real-world scenarios where it shines.

What Is the Computer Use Tool?

The Computer Use tool allows Gemini to receive a screenshot of a screen, reason about what it sees, and return a structured action — such as a mouse click at specific coordinates, keyboard input, or a scroll gesture. Your application then executes the action and feeds the next screenshot back to Gemini, creating an autonomous feedback loop.

Supported Action Types

| Action | Description | |--------|-------------| | click | Click at specified (x, y) coordinates | | type | Type a string of text | | key | Send a keyboard shortcut or key press | | scroll | Scroll in a given direction at given coordinates | | screenshot | Request a new screenshot (for loop continuation) | | move | Move the mouse cursor without clicking |

Supported Models

Computer Use is available on the following Gemini 3 models as of March 2026:

| Model | Characteristics | |-------|----------------| | gemini-3-flash-preview | Fast and cost-efficient. Ideal for agentic workflows | | gemini-3-pro-preview | Highest accuracy. Best for complex UI interactions |

Note: In the Gemini 2.5 series, Computer Use required a dedicated gemini-2.5-pro-preview-computer-use model. With Gemini 3, the capability is built directly into both Flash and Pro, simplifying your setup considerably.

Prerequisites

  • Basic Python knowledge
  • A Gemini API key (available via Google AI Studio)
  • A browser automation library: Playwright (local) or Browserbase (cloud)

Step 1: Installation

pip install google-genai playwright
playwright install chromium

Step 2: The Core Computer Use Loop

The heart of Computer Use is a simple loop: capture screenshot → send to Gemini → receive action → execute → repeat.

import base64
from google import genai
from google.genai import types

# Initialize the client
client = genai.Client(api_key="YOUR_API_KEY")

def encode_screenshot(image_bytes: bytes) -> str:
    """Encode screenshot bytes to base64 string"""
    return base64.b64encode(image_bytes).decode("utf-8")

def get_next_action(screenshot_b64: str, task: str, history: list):
    """
    Send the current screenshot to Gemini and get the next action.
    """
    # Define the Computer Use tool
    computer_use_tool = types.Tool(
        computer_use=types.ComputerUseTool(
            display_width=1280,
            display_height=800,
        )
    )

    # Build the message with screenshot + task description
    messages = history + [
        {
            "role": "user",
            "content": [
                {
                    "type": "image",
                    "source": {
                        "type": "base64",
                        "media_type": "image/png",
                        "data": screenshot_b64,
                    },
                },
                {
                    "type": "text",
                    "text": f"This is the current screen. Task: {task}\nWhat should I do next?",
                },
            ],
        }
    ]

    response = client.models.generate_content(
        model="gemini-3-flash-preview",
        contents=messages,
        config=types.GenerateContentConfig(
            tools=[computer_use_tool],
            system_instruction=(
                "You are an AI agent that controls a computer. "
                "Look at the screen and decide the best action to accomplish the user's task. "
                "When the task is complete, respond with 'TASK_COMPLETE'."
            ),
        ),
    )
    return response

Step 3: Building a Full Browser Agent with Playwright

import asyncio
from playwright.async_api import async_playwright

async def browser_agent(task: str):
    """An AI agent that autonomously controls a browser."""
    async with async_playwright() as p:
        browser = await p.chromium.launch(headless=False)
        page = await browser.new_page(viewport={"width": 1280, "height": 800})

        # Start from Google
        await page.goto("https://www.google.com")

        history = []
        max_steps = 15  # Safety limit to prevent infinite loops

        for step in range(max_steps):
            # Capture the current state of the browser
            screenshot_bytes = await page.screenshot()
            screenshot_b64 = encode_screenshot(screenshot_bytes)

            print(f"\n--- Step {step + 1} ---")

            # Ask Gemini what to do next
            response = get_next_action(screenshot_b64, task, history)

            # Parse the response
            action = None
            for part in response.candidates[0].content.parts:
                if hasattr(part, "text") and "TASK_COMPLETE" in part.text:
                    print("Task complete!")
                    await browser.close()
                    return
                if hasattr(part, "computer_use") and part.computer_use:
                    action = part.computer_use
                    break

            if not action:
                print("No action returned. Assuming task is complete.")
                break

            # Execute the action
            print(f"Executing: {action.action}")
            await execute_action(page, action)

            # Append to history so Gemini remembers context
            history.append({
                "role": "assistant",
                "content": response.candidates[0].content.parts,
            })

            # Brief pause between actions
            await asyncio.sleep(0.5)

        await browser.close()

async def execute_action(page, action):
    """Translate a Gemini Computer Use action into a Playwright command."""
    if action.action == "click":
        await page.mouse.click(action.coordinate[0], action.coordinate[1])
    elif action.action == "type":
        await page.keyboard.type(action.text)
    elif action.action == "key":
        await page.keyboard.press(action.key)
    elif action.action == "scroll":
        await page.mouse.wheel(
            action.coordinate[0],
            action.coordinate[1],
            delta_x=getattr(action, "delta_x", 0),
            delta_y=getattr(action, "delta_y", 0),
        )
    elif action.action == "move":
        await page.mouse.move(action.coordinate[0], action.coordinate[1])

# Run the agent
asyncio.run(
    browser_agent("Search for 'Gemini 3 API' on Google and open the first result.")
)

Real-World Use Cases

Use Case 1: Automated Form Filling

Stop copy-pasting data from spreadsheets into web forms. Let Gemini do it for you:

task = """
Fill out the registration form with the following details:
- Name: John Smith
- Email: john.smith@example.com
- Preferred date: April 15, 2026
Then click the Submit button.
"""
asyncio.run(browser_agent(task))

Use Case 2: Web Research Agent

Gemini can browse multiple pages, extract information, and synthesize findings:

task = """
1. Search Google for 'Gemini 3 release notes'
2. Open the official Google Developers Blog article
3. Read the key updates listed in the article
4. Once you have the information, respond with 'TASK_COMPLETE'
"""
asyncio.run(browser_agent(task))

Use Case 3: Automated UI Testing

Replace fragile selector-based tests with visual, human-like verification:

task = """
Test the login flow:
1. Go to http://localhost:3000/login
2. Enter 'test@example.com' in the username field and 'password123' in the password field
3. Click the Login button
4. Confirm the dashboard page has loaded
5. If successful, return 'TASK_COMPLETE'
"""
asyncio.run(browser_agent(task))

Best Practices

Write Specific Task Descriptions

Vague instructions lead to unpredictable behavior. Instead of "click the button," say "click the blue Submit button in the top-right corner of the form." The more specific you are, the more reliably Gemini will understand the goal.

Always Set a Maximum Step Limit

Never run the agent loop without a max_steps guard. Edge cases like captchas, unexpected pop-ups, or ambiguous UI states can otherwise lead to indefinite loops.

Add Confirmation Steps for Critical Actions

For irreversible actions — form submissions, purchases, deletions — insert a confirmation prompt before execution. This prevents unintended consequences when the AI misinterprets context.

Implement Robust Error Handling

Unexpected UI changes happen. Wrap action execution in try-except blocks and optionally feed the error state screenshot back to Gemini to let it self-recover:

try:
    await execute_action(page, action)
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Action failed: {e}")
    # Take an error screenshot and ask Gemini how to recover
    error_screenshot = await page.screenshot()
    recovery_response = get_next_action(
        encode_screenshot(error_screenshot),
        f"The previous action failed with: {e}. How should we recover?",
        history
    )

Frequently Asked Questions

Q: How is this different from Gemini 2.5's Computer Use? A: Gemini 2.5 required a separate dedicated model (gemini-2.5-pro-preview-computer-use). With Gemini 3, Computer Use is natively integrated into both Flash and Pro, so you don't need a separate model endpoint.

Q: Can I run this locally? A: Absolutely. With Playwright installed locally, the agent controls a real browser on your machine. For cloud-based execution, Browserbase is a popular option.

Q: Is there an additional charge for Computer Use? A: No extra fee. Standard Gemini API token pricing applies. Screenshots are billed as multimodal (image) inputs.

Q: What about security? Can the agent access sensitive data? A: The agent only sees what's on screen. Use sandboxed browser environments for sensitive workflows, and never run an untrusted task prompt with access to authenticated sessions containing sensitive information.

Q: Does it support video input instead of screenshots? A: Currently, the primary input is still screenshots. For fluid interaction, you can capture screenshots at high frequency. Native video support may come in future releases.

Conclusion

Gemini 3's Computer Use tool represents a major leap forward in AI agent capabilities. Whether you want to automate repetitive form-filling, power visual UI testing, or build a fully autonomous research assistant, the foundation is now accessible to any developer with a Gemini API key.

Start with gemini-3-flash-preview for rapid prototyping — its speed and cost make it the ideal model for iteration. When you need higher accuracy for complex, multi-step interfaces, upgrade to gemini-3-pro-preview.

The era of AI that can truly "use a computer" is here. What will you build?

Resources:

  • Official sample repo: google-gemini/computer-use-preview
  • Gemini 3 Flash Preview docs: Google AI for Developers
  • Google AI Studio (get your API key): aistudio.google.com

en.title: Gemini Deep Research Agent Guide — Building Autonomous Research with the Interactions API
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: Learn how to use Gemini's new Deep Research Agent via the Interactions API. Build autonomous multi-step research workflows with document analysis, web grounding, and cited report generation.
en.content:
What Is the Gemini Deep Research Agent?

In March 2026, Google launched the Deep Research Agent as a preview feature in the Gemini API. It is an AI agent capable of autonomously planning, executing, and synthesizing multi-step research tasks.

Unlike traditional RAG or search grounding, the Deep Research Agent can:

  • Automatically create multi-step research plans
  • Gather information across both the web and provided documents
  • Generate detailed reports with full citations
  • Run asynchronously in the background for long-duration tasks

This gives you an end-to-end autonomous research capability in a single API call.

Requirements

The Deep Research Agent is only accessible via the Interactions API (Beta).

  • Underlying model: gemini-3.1-pro
  • API: Interactions API (Beta)
  • Execution: Asynchronous background execution

Setup

First, update the google-genai package to the latest version:

pip install --upgrade google-genai

Basic Usage

1. Simple Research Request

import google.genai as genai
import time

client = genai.Client(api_key="YOUR_API_KEY")

# Launch the Deep Research Agent via Interactions API
interaction = client.interactions.create(
    agent="deep-research",
    contents="Create a performance comparison report of major generative AI models in 2026. Include Gemini, GPT, and Claude with benchmark results and key differentiators.",
    config={
        "background": True  # Required for async execution
    }
)

print(f"Interaction ID: {interaction.id}")
print(f"Status: {interaction.status}")

# Poll for completion
while interaction.status not in ["completed", "failed"]:
    time.sleep(10)
    interaction = client.interactions.get(interaction.id)
    print(f"Status: {interaction.status}")

# Retrieve results
if interaction.status == "completed":
    print(interaction.result.text)

2. Research with Document Uploads

You can upload local PDF or CSV files and combine them with live web data.

import pathlib

# Upload a document
uploaded_file = client.files.upload(
    path=pathlib.Path("market_report_2025.pdf"),
    config={"mime_type": "application/pdf"}
)

# Run research combining the file and web search
interaction = client.interactions.create(
    agent="deep-research",
    contents=[
        uploaded_file,
        "Using the attached report and the latest web information, create a 2026 AI market trend analysis."
    ],
    config={
        "background": True,
        "tools": ["file_search", "google_search"]
    }
)

Production-Grade Async Pattern

For long-running research tasks, an async polling pattern is recommended over blocking calls.

import asyncio
import google.genai as genai

async def run_deep_research(query: str) -> str:
    client = genai.AsyncClient(api_key="YOUR_API_KEY")

    # Start research
    interaction = await client.interactions.create(
        agent="deep-research",
        contents=query,
        config={"background": True}
    )

    # Wait for completion (max 30 minutes)
    max_wait = 1800
    elapsed = 0
    poll_interval = 15

    while elapsed < max_wait:
        await asyncio.sleep(poll_interval)
        elapsed += poll_interval

        interaction = await client.interactions.get(interaction.id)

        if interaction.status == "completed":
            return interaction.result.text
        elif interaction.status == "failed":
            raise RuntimeError(f"Research failed: {interaction.error}")

    raise TimeoutError("Research task timed out")

# Usage
result = asyncio.run(run_deep_research(
    "Create a usage guide comparing Gemini 3 series models by use case."
))
print(result)

Customizing Output

JSON Schema Output

Specify a response_schema to receive structured results:

from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import List

class Source(BaseModel):
    title: str
    url: str
    relevance: float

class ResearchReport(BaseModel):
    summary: str
    key_findings: List[str]
    sources: List[Source]
    confidence_score: float

interaction = client.interactions.create(
    agent="deep-research",
    contents="Research the current state of quantum computing commercialization in 2026.",
    config={
        "background": True,
        "response_schema": ResearchReport
    }
)

Control Output Structure via Prompt

You can also define the output format directly in your prompt without a schema:

interaction = client.interactions.create(
    agent="deep-research",
    contents="""
    Research the latest Gemini API pricing and produce a report in the following format:

    ## Executive Summary
    (3 sentences max)

    ## Pricing Table by Model
    | Model | Input | Output | Notes |

    ## Cost Optimization Tips
    (5 bullet points)

    ## References
    (URLs with retrieval dates)
    """,
    config={"background": True}
)

Pricing

Deep Research Agent costs depend on the depth of research the agent autonomously performs.

| Component | Billing Basis | |-----------|--------------| | Input tokens | Gemini 3.1 Pro rate ($1.25/M tokens) | | Output tokens | Gemini 3.1 Pro rate ($10.00/M tokens) | | Web searches | Per search call | | File searches | Based on file size and processing |

Since the agent runs multiple searches across multiple steps, clearly scoping your research request in the prompt is the most effective way to control costs.

Deep Research Agent vs. generate_content

| Feature | generate_content | Deep Research Agent | |---------|----------------|---------------------| | Execution | Synchronous | Async background | | Autonomous planning | No | Yes (multi-step) | | Web search | Manual configuration | Auto-determined | | Document analysis | Manual | Automatic cross-referencing | | Execution time | Seconds | Minutes to tens of minutes | | Cost | Low–Medium | Medium–High (complexity-dependent) |

Practical Use Cases

Automated Competitive Intelligence Schedule regular competitor analysis and deliver reports to Slack or email automatically.

Technology Trend Monitoring Survey the latest developments in a specific domain daily and aggregate into a dashboard.

Regulatory Change Tracking Monitor changes in industry regulations automatically and alert compliance teams.

Investment Research Assistance Automate the initial research phase of company analysis (with humans making final decisions).

Important Caveats

  • The Deep Research Agent is currently in preview — use with caution in production
  • Always fact-check research results; accuracy is not guaranteed
  • Background execution requires polling or webhooks to retrieve results

Conclusion

The Gemini Deep Research Agent is a powerful feature that automates complex, multi-step research workflows through a single API. Combined with the Interactions API's background execution, it dramatically simplifies automated report generation and information aggregation. Take advantage of the preview period to explore use cases and prepare for production adoption.

en.title: Complete Guide to Gemini 2.5 Flash — The Hybrid Model That Balances Speed and Reasoning
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: A thorough guide to Gemini 2.5 Flash's features, pricing, and API usage. Learn how to control reasoning depth with thinking_budget for optimal cost and performance.
en.content:
Introduction

Gemini 2.5 Flash is Google's next-generation AI model that achieves an exceptional balance of speed, cost-efficiency, and reasoning capability. Since its release in 2025, it has earned strong praise from developers — particularly for its hybrid design that lets you toggle "thinking" on or off as needed.

In this guide, we'll walk through Gemini 2.5 Flash's key features, how to use the API, and practical strategies for managing cost.

What Is Gemini 2.5 Flash?

Gemini 2.5 Flash is developed by Google DeepMind and sits in the fastest, most cost-efficient tier of the Gemini 2.5 series. It's not simply a "lite" version of the Pro model — it delivers reasoning capability that approaches Gemini 2.5 Pro while dramatically reducing latency and cost.

Key Features

  • Hybrid reasoning model: The world's first production-ready model allowing you to toggle thinking via the thinking_budget parameter
  • 1M token context window: Process long documents, codebases, and conversation history in full
  • Multimodal input: Handles text, images, video, audio, and PDFs seamlessly
  • Low cost: Significantly cheaper than Gemini 2.5 Pro

Specifications

| Item | Value | |------|-------| | Context window | 1,048,576 tokens | | Max output tokens | 65,536 tokens | | Input pricing | $0.15 / 1M tokens (no thinking) | | Output pricing | $0.60 / 1M tokens (no thinking) | | Thinking token pricing | $0.35 / 1M tokens | | Supported modalities | Text / Image / Video / Audio / PDF |

Prerequisites

Before diving in, it helps to have the following:

  • Basic familiarity with Python or JavaScript
  • Experience using REST APIs or SDKs
  • An API key (available for free from Google AI Studio)

Setup

Install the Python SDK

pip install google-generativeai

Set Your Environment Variable

export GEMINI_API_KEY="your_api_key_here"

Basic Usage

Text Generation (Without Thinking)

The simplest use case is specifying the gemini-2.5-flash model and generating text.

import google.generativeai as genai
import os

genai.configure(api_key=os.environ["GEMINI_API_KEY"])

model = genai.GenerativeModel("gemini-2.5-flash")

response = model.generate_content("Write a Python function to check if a number is prime.")
print(response.text)

Streaming Responses

For long outputs, use streaming to receive content in real time.

import google.generativeai as genai
import os

genai.configure(api_key=os.environ["GEMINI_API_KEY"])

model = genai.GenerativeModel("gemini-2.5-flash")

for chunk in model.generate_content("Explain quantum computing in detail.", stream=True):
    print(chunk.text, end="", flush=True)

print()  # newline

Using the Thinking Feature

The standout capability of Gemini 2.5 Flash is the thinking_budget parameter. By setting a maximum number of tokens for internal reasoning, you can precisely balance quality against cost and latency.

thinking_budget Reference

| Value | Behavior | |-------|----------| | 0 | No thinking (fastest, cheapest) | | 1–1024 | Light reasoning | | 1025–8192 | Moderate reasoning | | 8193–24576 | Deep reasoning (highest quality) |

Setting a thinking_budget

import google.generativeai as genai
import os

genai.configure(api_key=os.environ["GEMINI_API_KEY"])

model = genai.GenerativeModel("gemini-2.5-flash")

# Enable deep thinking (ideal for math and reasoning tasks)
response = model.generate_content(
    "Solve the equation: x^3 - 6x^2 + 11x - 6 = 0",
    generation_config=genai.GenerationConfig(
        thinking_config=genai.types.ThinkingConfig(
            thinking_budget=8192
        )
    )
)

print("Thinking tokens used:", response.usage_metadata.thoughts_token_count)
print("Answer:", response.text)

Inspecting the Thinking Process

Setting include_thoughts=True lets you retrieve the model's internal reasoning — useful for debugging or educational purposes.

import google.generativeai as genai
import os

genai.configure(api_key=os.environ["GEMINI_API_KEY"])

model = genai.GenerativeModel("gemini-2.5-flash")

response = model.generate_content(
    "What is the shortest flight route from Tokyo to New York?",
    generation_config=genai.GenerationConfig(
        thinking_config=genai.types.ThinkingConfig(
            thinking_budget=4096,
            include_thoughts=True
        )
    )
)

for part in response.candidates[0].content.parts:
    if part.thought:
        print("=== Thinking Process ===")
        print(part.text)
    else:
        print("=== Final Answer ===")
        print(part.text)

Multimodal Input

Gemini 2.5 Flash can process far more than text.

Image Analysis

import google.generativeai as genai
import PIL.Image
import os

genai.configure(api_key=os.environ["GEMINI_API_KEY"])

model = genai.GenerativeModel("gemini-2.5-flash")

image = PIL.Image.open("chart.png")

response = model.generate_content([
    "Analyze this chart and describe the key trends.",
    image
])

print(response.text)

PDF Analysis

import google.generativeai as genai
import os

genai.configure(api_key=os.environ["GEMINI_API_KEY"])

pdf_file = genai.upload_file("report.pdf", mime_type="application/pdf")

model = genai.GenerativeModel("gemini-2.5-flash")

response = model.generate_content([
    "Identify three key takeaways from this report and explain each one.",
    pdf_file
])

print(response.text)

Cost Optimization Best Practices

Recommended thinking_budget by Use Case

| Use Case | Recommended Budget | Reason | |----------|-------------------|--------| | Chat / Q&A | 0–1024 | Speed matters most | | Code generation | 2048–8192 | Logical consistency required | | Math / Puzzles | 8192–24576 | Deep reasoning is beneficial | | Document summarization | 0–512 | Simple extraction task | | Agents / Planning | 4096–16384 | Multi-step planning needed |

Context Caching

If you're reusing the same system prompt repeatedly, leverage context caching — cached input tokens are billed at 25% of the standard rate, which can significantly reduce costs in high-volume applications.

FAQ

Q: When should I choose Gemini 2.5 Flash over Gemini 2.5 Pro?

A: Choose Flash when cost and speed are priorities. For tasks requiring the absolute highest accuracy — such as advanced coding or complex multi-step reasoning — Pro may be more appropriate. That said, raising Flash's thinking_budget can bring it very close to Pro quality for many tasks.

Q: What's the difference between thinking_budget=0 and just not using thinking?

A: Setting thinking_budget=0 explicitly disables the thinking process, resulting in the fastest and cheapest mode of operation. This is ideal for simple Q&A, text transformation, or any task that doesn't require deep reasoning.

Q: Can I really use the full 1M token context window?

A: Yes. However, latency and cost increase with token count. It's most valuable for processing long documents or large codebases, but you should avoid unnecessarily long contexts to keep things efficient.

Q: Can I call it via REST without an SDK?

A: Absolutely. You can use cURL or any HTTP client. The endpoint is https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/models/gemini-2.5-flash:generateContent.

Summary

Gemini 2.5 Flash stands among the best available models in terms of balancing speed, cost, and reasoning power. The thinking_budget feature gives you fine-grained control to match model behavior to your task complexity, enabling substantial cost savings.

With broad support for chat, agents, document analysis, and multimodal workflows, a solid strategy is to start with Flash and upgrade to Pro only when the task truly demands it — keeping development costs low from the start.

References

  • Gemini 2.5 Flash — Google AI for Developers
  • Gemini API — Google AI Studio
  • Start building with Gemini 2.5 Flash — Google Developers Blog

en.title: Getting Started with Gemini CLI — Master Gemini from Your Terminal
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: Master Gemini CLI for terminal-based AI development. Code generation, file analysis, and shell integration guide.
en.content:
Harness AI Power from Your Terminal with Gemini CLI

Gemini CLI brings the capabilities of the Gemini API directly to your terminal, enabling code generation, analysis, documentation, and workflow automation without leaving the command line. Whether you're reviewing code, generating documentation, or integrating AI into shell scripts, Gemini CLI streamlines your development process.

Installation and Setup

Installing Gemini CLI

Install Gemini CLI globally using npm:

# Global installation (recommended)
npm install -g @anthropic-ai/gemini-cli

# Verify installation
gemini --version

Configuring Your API Key

Before using Gemini CLI, you need a Gemini API key:

# Set via environment variable (recommended)
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-api-key-here"

# Or configure globally
gemini config set api-key "your-api-key-here"

# Verify configuration
gemini config show

For local development, use a .env file:

# Create .env file
echo "GEMINI_API_KEY=your-api-key-here" > .env

# Add to .gitignore (CRITICAL!)
echo ".env" >> .gitignore

Core Commands

Text Generation

Generate text from command-line prompts:

# Simple text generation
gemini prompt "Explain asynchronous programming in Python"

# Multi-line prompt
gemini prompt "
Optimize this function:
def calculate_sum(numbers):
    total = 0
    for num in numbers:
        total = total + num
    return total
"

# Generate with specific model
gemini prompt "Write a REST API endpoint" --model gemini-2.5-pro

File Analysis

# Analyze code for issues
gemini analyze app.py

# Diagnose errors with suggestions
gemini analyze error.log --analyze-errors

# Batch analyze multiple files
gemini analyze src/*.py --summary

Code Generation

# Generate code in specific language
gemini generate "REST API with FastAPI" --lang python

# Generate multiple related files
gemini generate "User authentication system" --lang python --output-dir generated/

# Use predefined templates
gemini generate-from-template --template express-api

Practical Use Cases

Use Case 1: Code Review and Refactoring

Automatically review code for improvements:

# Review code quality
gemini review my-script.py

# Focus on specific aspects
gemini review my-script.py --focus security

# Generate detailed report
gemini review src/ --detailed --output code-review.md

Example output:

$ gemini review data_processor.py --focus performance
Analyzing: data_processor.py

Performance Issues Found:
1. Line 45: Nested loops can use list comprehension
2. Line 78: Multiple iterations - consolidate into single pass
3. Line 92: Regex compiled in loop - move to module level

Recommendations:
- Use NumPy for vectorized operations (10x speedup potential)
- Implement caching for expensive calculations
- Consider generator expressions for memory efficiency

Estimated impact: 0.8 tokens/execution

Use Case 2: Documentation Generation

# Auto-generate API documentation
gemini doc generate src/api/ --format markdown

# Create README with analysis
gemini doc create-readme --project "Python data pipeline" --features streaming processing

# Generate OpenAPI spec
gemini doc api-docs src/routes/ --format openapi

Example:

$ gemini doc api-docs app/routes/ --format openapi
Generated: openapi-spec.yaml

Endpoints documented:
✓ POST /api/users (Create user)
✓ GET /api/users/{id} (Fetch user)
✓ PUT /api/users/{id} (Update user)
✓ DELETE /api/users/{id} (Delete user)

Includes:
✓ Request/response schemas
✓ Authentication details
✓ Error responses
✓ Rate limiting info

Use Case 3: Shell Workflow Integration

Integrate Gemini CLI into bash scripts for automated pipelines:

#!/bin/bash
# build-and-optimize.sh

echo "🔨 Building project..."
npm run build || exit 1

echo "🧪 Running tests..."
npm test || exit 1

echo "📊 Analyzing code quality..."
gemini review src/ --focus quality --output reports/code-quality.md

echo "📝 Generating documentation..."
gemini doc generate src/ --output docs/api.md

echo "✅ Pipeline complete!"

Run it:

chmod +x build-and-optimize.sh
./build-and-optimize.sh

Use Case 4: Batch File Processing

# Analyze all Python files in a directory
for file in src/**/*.py; do
  echo "Processing $file..."
  gemini analyze "$file" --output "reports/$(basename $file .py).txt"
done

# Process with pipe input
cat problematic-logs.txt | gemini prompt "Extract all critical errors"

# Chain multiple operations
cat requirements.txt | gemini prompt "Generate installation and usage docs"

Advanced Features

Interactive Chat Mode

Multi-turn conversations in the terminal:

# Start interactive chat
gemini chat

# Chat with system context
gemini chat --system "You are a Python expert"

# Persist conversation history
gemini chat --history my-conversation.json

Chat example:

$ gemini chat --system "Data scientist"

Chat started (type 'quit' to exit)

You: What are best practices for data cleaning with Pandas?
Assistant: Here are the key practices for data cleaning:

1. Handle missing values
   - Use `isnull()` to detect
   - Use `dropna()` or `fillna()` to handle

2. Remove duplicates
   - Use `drop_duplicates()`
...

You: Show me code examples
Assistant: ```python
import pandas as pd

# Load data
df = pd.read_csv('data.csv')

# Check missing values
print(df.isnull().sum())

# Drop rows with missing values
df = df.dropna()

# Fill missing values
df['age'].fillna(df['age'].mean(), inplace=True)
...

You: quit Conversation saved to my-conversation.json


### CI/CD Pipeline Integration

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# ci-checks.sh

# Run tests
npm test || exit 1

# Code quality check (fail on critical issues)
gemini review src/ --fail-on-critical --output quality.md

# Security scan
gemini security-scan src/ --strict || exit 1

# Generate test coverage report
gemini analyze coverage/index.json --format html --output coverage.html

echo "✅ All checks passed"

Custom Prompts and Templates

# Create custom prompt templates
mkdir -p ~/.gemini/prompts

# Save reusable prompt
cat > ~/.gemini/prompts/security-review.txt << 'EOF'
Review the following code for security vulnerabilities:

1. Authentication and authorization issues
2. Input validation problems
3. Data exposure risks
4. Injection attack vectors
5. Dependency vulnerabilities
EOF

# Use template
gemini prompt "$(cat ~/.gemini/prompts/security-review.txt)" < mycode.py

Complete Development Workflow

End-to-End Feature Development

#!/bin/bash
# feature-workflow.sh

FEATURE="user-authentication"
TIMESTAMP=$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S)

echo "🚀 Starting feature: $FEATURE"

# 1. Create implementation plan
echo "📋 Generating implementation plan..."
gemini prompt "Design a $FEATURE system with architecture, database schema, and API endpoints" \
  > docs/plan-$FEATURE-$TIMESTAMP.md

# 2. Generate boilerplate
echo "🤖 Generating code..."
gemini generate "Express.js with JWT $FEATURE" --lang javascript --output src/$FEATURE/

# 3. Generate tests
echo "✅ Generating tests..."
gemini generate "Unit tests for $FEATURE system" --lang javascript --output test/$FEATURE/

# 4. Security review
echo "🔒 Security check..."
gemini review src/$FEATURE/ --focus security --output reports/security-$TIMESTAMP.md

# 5. Generate docs
echo "📖 Documentation..."
gemini doc generate src/$FEATURE/ --output docs/api-$FEATURE.md

# 6. Performance analysis
echo "⚡ Performance analysis..."
gemini analyze src/$FEATURE/ --stats --output reports/stats-$TIMESTAMP.json

echo "✨ Feature development complete!"
echo "📁 Review reports in: reports/"

Run the workflow:

chmod +x feature-workflow.sh
./feature-workflow.sh

Performance and Optimization

Caching for Efficiency

# Enable prompt caching
gemini config cache-prompts on

# Check cache status
gemini cache status

# Clear cache if needed
gemini cache clear

Parallel Processing

# Analyze multiple files in parallel
gemini batch-analyze src/ \
  --pattern "*.py" \
  --output-dir reports/ \
  --parallel 4  # Process 4 files simultaneously

Troubleshooting

Common Issues and Solutions

# Issue: API key not found
# Solution: Verify key configuration
gemini config show | grep api-key

# Issue: Network timeout
# Solution: Retry with exponential backoff
gemini prompt "..." --retry 3 --retry-delay 2000

# Issue: Rate limiting
# Solution: Reduce parallelism
gemini batch-analyze src/ --parallel 1 --delay 1000

# Issue: Token limit exceeded
# Solution: Process smaller chunks
gemini analyze large-file.py --max-tokens 100000 --chunk-size 50000

Best Practices

  1. Environment Management: Store API keys in environment variables or .env files, never in code
  2. Output Preservation: Redirect CLI output to files for audit trails and reference
  3. Error Handling: Use || in scripts to handle failures gracefully
  4. Incremental Integration: Start with simple tasks, gradually add complexity
  5. Token Optimization: Monitor token usage, avoid redundant API calls
  6. Version Control: Include .gemini config files in repos when appropriate (without secrets)

Summary

Gemini CLI empowers developers to leverage AI capabilities seamlessly within their existing terminal workflows. From code generation and review to documentation and CI/CD integration, Gemini CLI accelerates development velocity while maintaining code quality and security standards.

Start small—try analyzing a single file—and gradually expand to full workflow automation. You'll quickly discover how Gemini CLI can become an indispensable part of your development toolkit.

en.title: Implementing Streaming Responses and Multi-turn Chat with Gemini API
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: Master streaming responses and multi-turn conversations with Gemini API. Complete Python guide with production-ready examples.
en.content:
Building Real-time Chat Experiences with Gemini API

Creating engaging user experiences requires two essential capabilities: streaming responses that display text progressively, and multi-turn conversations that preserve context across exchanges. This guide covers implementing both features with the Gemini API.

Understanding Streaming Responses

Standard Non-Streaming Response

First, let's examine the baseline approach without streaming:

from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic()

# Standard response (wait for complete text)
response = client.messages.create(
    model="gemini-2.5-pro",
    max_tokens=1024,
    messages=[
        {
            "role": "user",
            "content": "Implement quicksort algorithm in Python with detailed comments"
        }
    ]
)

print(response.content[0].text)

This works, but users must wait for the entire response before seeing anything.

Implementing Streaming Responses

Streaming returns text in chunks as it becomes available:

from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic()

# Enable streaming with context manager
with client.messages.stream(
    model="gemini-2.5-pro",
    max_tokens=1024,
    messages=[
        {
            "role": "user",
            "content": "Implement quicksort algorithm in Python with detailed comments"
        }
    ]
) as stream:
    # Text arrives in real-time
    for text in stream.text_stream:
        print(text, end="", flush=True)
    print()  # Newline after completion

# Access final message and token usage
final_message = stream.get_final_message()
print(f"Total tokens used: {final_message.usage.input_tokens + final_message.usage.output_tokens}")

Key Points:

  • Use client.messages.stream() context manager for streaming
  • Text arrives in chunks requiring multiple iterations
  • flush=True ensures immediate display of each chunk
  • get_final_message() provides complete metrics after streaming completes

Advanced Chunk Processing

For more sophisticated control over streaming:

from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic()

def stream_with_tracking(messages, model="gemini-2.5-pro"):
    """Stream responses and track detailed metrics"""
    full_response = ""
    chunk_count = 0
    chunk_sizes = []

    with client.messages.stream(
        model=model,
        max_tokens=2000,
        messages=messages
    ) as stream:
        for text in stream.text_stream:
            full_response += text
            chunk_count += 1
            chunk_sizes.append(len(text))

            # Progress indicator every 100 chunks
            if chunk_count % 100 == 0:
                print(f"[{chunk_count} chunks received...]")

    final_message = stream.get_final_message()
    usage = final_message.usage

    return {
        "response": full_response,
        "chunks": chunk_count,
        "avg_chunk_size": sum(chunk_sizes) / len(chunk_sizes),
        "input_tokens": usage.input_tokens,
        "output_tokens": usage.output_tokens
    }

# Usage example
result = stream_with_tracking([
    {
        "role": "user",
        "content": "Explain the scientific method in comprehensive detail"
    }
])

print(f"Response length: {len(result['response'])} characters")
print(f"Chunks: {result['chunks']}, Avg size: {result['avg_chunk_size']:.1f} chars")

Multi-turn Conversation Implementation

Building a Chat Session Class

from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic()

class ChatSession:
    def __init__(self, system_prompt=None):
        self.conversation_history = []
        self.system_prompt = system_prompt or "You are a helpful AI assistant."
        self.model = "gemini-2.5-pro"

    def add_message(self, role, content):
        """Add message to conversation history"""
        self.conversation_history.append({
            "role": role,
            "content": content
        })

    def send_message(self, user_message):
        """Send user message and get response"""
        self.add_message("user", user_message)

        # Send full conversation history to API
        response = client.messages.create(
            model=self.model,
            max_tokens=1024,
            system=self.system_prompt,
            messages=self.conversation_history
        )

        assistant_message = response.content[0].text
        self.add_message("assistant", assistant_message)

        return assistant_message

    def get_history(self):
        """Retrieve full conversation history"""
        return self.conversation_history

    def clear_history(self):
        """Reset conversation"""
        self.conversation_history = []

# Example usage
chat = ChatSession(
    system_prompt="You are an expert Python programmer who explains concepts clearly with code examples."
)

# Turn 1
response1 = chat.send_message("Explain list comprehensions in Python")
print(f"Q: Explain list comprehensions\nA: {response1}\n")

# Turn 2 (maintains context)
response2 = chat.send_message("Show me 5 practical examples")
print(f"Q: Show me 5 practical examples\nA: {response2}\n")

# Turn 3 (still maintains context from previous turns)
response3 = chat.send_message("What about performance benefits?")
print(f"Q: What about performance benefits?\nA: {response3}\n")

Combining Streaming with Multi-turn

class StreamingChatSession(ChatSession):
    """Chat session with streaming support"""

    def send_message_streaming(self, user_message):
        """Send message with streaming response"""
        self.add_message("user", user_message)

        full_response = ""

        with client.messages.stream(
            model=self.model,
            max_tokens=1024,
            system=self.system_prompt,
            messages=self.conversation_history
        ) as stream:
            for text in stream.text_stream:
                full_response += text
                print(text, end="", flush=True)

        print()  # Newline after streaming completes

        self.add_message("assistant", full_response)
        return full_response

# Usage
streaming_chat = StreamingChatSession(
    system_prompt="You are a data science expert. Explain concepts thoroughly with examples."
)

print("Question 1:")
streaming_chat.send_message_streaming("What's the difference between machine learning and deep learning?")

print("\nQuestion 2:")
streaming_chat.send_message_streaming("Tell me more about neural networks")

print("\nQuestion 3:")
streaming_chat.send_message_streaming("How do they work with unstructured data?")

System Prompts and Conversation Control

Designing Effective System Prompts

System prompts are the most powerful way to control AI behavior:

# Example: Tech Blog Writer
tech_writer_system = """
You are an experienced tech blogger. Follow these principles:

1. Explain complex concepts in beginner-friendly language
2. Include practical code examples
3. Provide actionable, implementable advice
4. Always encourage learning and growth
5. Be honest when you don't know something
"""

# Example: Data Analysis Consultant
analyst_system = """
You are a senior data analyst. Practice these habits:

1. Prioritize data-driven decision making
2. Ask clarifying questions about data quality
3. Focus on business value and ROI
4. Suggest multiple analytical approaches
5. Always document assumptions and limitations
"""

# Dynamic expert system prompts
def create_expert_system(domain, expertise_level="senior"):
    return f"""
You are a {expertise_level}-level expert in {domain}. Follow these guidelines:

1. Provide insights grounded in current industry best practices
2. Offer only verifiable, fact-based information
3. Clearly state when you're making educated guesses or extrapolations
4. Present multiple perspectives on complex issues
5. Explain thoroughly so the user develops deep understanding
"""

# Usage
finance_chat = ChatSession(
    system_prompt=create_expert_system("corporate finance", "senior")
)

Intelligent Context Management

Token-Aware History Management

Long conversations accumulate tokens and increase costs:

class ContextAwareChatSession(ChatSession):
    def __init__(self, system_prompt=None, max_context_tokens=4000):
        super().__init__(system_prompt)
        self.max_context_tokens = max_context_tokens

    def estimate_tokens(self, text):
        """Rough token estimation (1 word ≈ 1.3 tokens)"""
        return int(len(text.split()) * 1.3)

    def trim_history(self):
        """Trim old messages if history exceeds token limit"""
        estimated = self.estimate_tokens(str(self.conversation_history))

        while estimated > self.max_context_tokens and len(self.conversation_history) > 1:
            self.conversation_history.pop(0)
            estimated = self.estimate_tokens(str(self.conversation_history))

        return len(self.conversation_history)

    def send_message(self, user_message):
        """Send message with automatic history management"""
        self.add_message("user", user_message)

        messages_kept = self.trim_history()
        print(f"[Context: {messages_kept} messages in history]")

        response = client.messages.create(
            model=self.model,
            max_tokens=1024,
            system=self.system_prompt,
            messages=self.conversation_history
        )

        assistant_message = response.content[0].text
        self.add_message("assistant", assistant_message)

        return assistant_message

# Long conversation with automatic memory management
context_chat = ContextAwareChatSession(
    system_prompt="Helpful assistant",
    max_context_tokens=3000
)

# System automatically trims old messages as needed
for i in range(20):
    response = context_chat.send_message(f"Question {i+1}: Continue our discussion")

Production-Ready Application

Interactive Coding Assistant

def interactive_coding_assistant():
    """Production-grade coding assistant"""
    chat = StreamingChatSession(
        system_prompt="""
You are a senior software engineer and coding mentor.

Guidelines:
- Provide clean, efficient code examples following best practices
- Explain the reasoning behind your suggestions
- Consider performance, maintainability, and readability
- Help developers understand not just what code does, but why
- Suggest improvements to code posted by users
"""
    )

    print("=== Interactive Coding Assistant ===")
    print("Type 'quit' to exit, 'clear' to reset conversation\n")

    while True:
        user_input = input("You: ").strip()

        if user_input.lower() == 'quit':
            print("Goodbye!")
            break

        if user_input.lower() == 'clear':
            chat.clear_history()
            print("Conversation cleared.\n")
            continue

        if user_input:
            print("Assistant: ", end="")
            chat.send_message_streaming(user_input)
            print()

# Run the assistant
if __name__ == "__main__":
    interactive_coding_assistant()

Best Practices

  1. Strategic Streaming Use: Enable streaming only when UI needs real-time display
  2. System Prompt Optimization: Craft system prompts to control behavior reliably
  3. Context Management: Monitor token usage and prune old messages in long conversations
  4. Error Handling: Implement retry logic for streaming interruptions
  5. Cost Monitoring: Track API usage to manage expenses, avoid redundant requests
  6. User Experience: Display loading indicators while streaming; separate user input from AI responses

Summary

The combination of streaming responses and multi-turn conversations creates responsive, contextual AI applications. By mastering these capabilities with the Gemini API, you can build chat interfaces that feel natural and maintain meaningful dialogue across multiple turns.

en.title: Gemini 2.5 Pro vs 2.5 Flash — Model Selection Guide by Use Case
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: Compare Gemini 2.5 Pro and Flash models. Explore performance, speed, cost, context windows, and practical use cases.
en.content:
Choosing Between Gemini 2.5 Pro and Flash

Google's Gemini model family offers multiple variants designed for different use cases. Understanding the performance and cost tradeoffs between Gemini 2.5 Pro and the fast Gemini 2.5 Flash is essential for building efficient AI-powered applications.

Core Model Characteristics

Gemini 2.5 Pro Strengths

Gemini 2.5 Pro is engineered for advanced reasoning and complex task processing with these defining features:

  • Reasoning Capability: Excels at complex logic problems, mathematical reasoning, and multi-step problem solving
  • Context Window: 200,000 tokens supports analysis of lengthy documents, codebases, and research papers
  • Code Generation: Superior ability to generate, debug, and optimize software code with architectural understanding
  • Multimodal Analysis: Handles complex tasks involving images, videos, and audio with sophisticated interpretation

Response latency averages 3-5 seconds, making it ideal when accuracy is the priority.

Gemini 2.5 Flash Strengths

Gemini 2.5 Flash prioritizes speed and efficiency for real-time applications:

  • Response Speed: Average latency of 0.5-1 second—5 to 10 times faster than Pro
  • Cost Efficiency: API pricing is approximately 60-70% lower per token compared to Pro
  • Context Window: 100,000 tokens covers most practical use cases effectively
  • Optimized Inference: Fine-tuned for scenarios requiring immediate responses

Detailed Performance Comparison

| Metric | Gemini 2.5 Pro | Gemini 2.5 Flash | |--------|---|---| | Context Window | 200,000 tokens | 100,000 tokens | | Avg Response Time | 3-5 seconds | 0.5-1 second | | Reasoning Quality | Exceptional | High | | Input Cost (per M tokens) | $7.50 | $2.50 | | Output Cost (per M tokens) | $30.00 | $10.00 | | Complex Reasoning | Excellent | Strong | | Standard Text Tasks | Overkill | Sufficient |

When to Choose Gemini 2.5 Pro

Complex Mathematical Problem Solving

For research-grade mathematics involving intricate notation and multi-step derivations:

from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic()

# Solve complex differential equations
response = client.messages.create(
    model="gemini-2.5-pro",
    max_tokens=2048,
    messages=[
        {
            "role": "user",
            "content": "Solve the differential equation: d²y/dx² - 4dy/dx + 4y = e^(2x)"
        }
    ]
)

print(response.content[0].text)

Software Architecture Design Consultation

When synthesizing multiple requirements into a scalable system design:

# Multi-turn conversation for iterative design
conversation_history = [
    {
        "role": "user",
        "content": "Design a backend architecture for a social media platform serving 1M users. Consider scalability, data consistency, and failure modes."
    }
]

response = client.messages.create(
    model="gemini-2.5-pro",
    max_tokens=3000,
    messages=conversation_history
)

# Follow up with architectural specifics
conversation_history.append({
    "role": "assistant",
    "content": response.content[0].text
})

conversation_history.append({
    "role": "user",
    "content": "Elaborate on the real-time notification system. How would you handle delivery guarantees across multiple regions?"
})

response = client.messages.create(
    model="gemini-2.5-pro",
    max_tokens=2500,
    messages=conversation_history
)

Comprehensive Document Analysis

Leverage the 200,000 token context to analyze entire research papers or technical specifications in a single request:

# Analyze large technical documents
large_document = read_entire_pdf()  # ~150K tokens

response = client.messages.create(
    model="gemini-2.5-pro",
    max_tokens=2000,
    messages=[
